3A_Bibliographic.citation: Kiss, O., Nagata, J. M., de Zambotti, M., Dick, A. S., Marshall, A. T., Sowell, E. R., Van Rinsveld, A., Guillaume, M., Pelham, W. E., III, Gonzalez, R., Brown, S. A., Dowling, G. J., Lisdahl, K. M., Tapert, S. F., & Baker, F. C. (2023). Effects of the Covid-19 pandemic on screen time and sleep in early adolescents. Health Psychology, 42(12), 894-903.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: ABCD 3.0 data release and ABCD COVID-19 Survey First and Second Data Release (DOI: 10.15154/1520584 and 10.15154/1522601)
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Data included here are from 5,027 adolescents (2,424 girls and 2,603 boys), who completed the 2-year annual follow-up visit (pre-pandemic period; age range: 10-13 years, mean = 11.94 year; 3.0 release: 2018-2020) and at least one COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Survey (COVID-19 assessments; age range: 11-14 years).

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Time spent on social media platforms (minutes/day) and single player video game time (minutes/day)
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: Youth Screen Time Survey (pre-pandemic) and COVID-19 survey screen time items
3C_Exposure.modalities: social media, single player video games
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (minutes/day); standardized for models; weighted average of weekday and schoolday for pre-pandemic; past week for COVID surveys
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Pre-pandemic screen time was determined for the Year 2 follow-up visit using the Youth Screen Time Survey. Participants answered questions about typical hours per day (not including school-related activities, separately for school days and weekend days) spent on six screen activities (internet browsing, texting, video chatting, social media use, playing single player, and playing multiplayer, video games). We reduced the six screen time variables to the items that had the lowest correlation (Spearman's rho = 0.13): time spent with social media use and single player video game time, as representatives for screen time.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Sleep measures: time in bed (hours), bedtime, sleep onset latency (minutes), number of awakenings
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Munich Chronotype Questionnaire (MCTQ - youth version) items from Year 2 and harmonized COVID-19 survey sleep items
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: We used items belonging to a subscale of the Munich Chronotype Questionnaire (MCTQ - youth version) that were included in the original ABCD Study (Year 2) and compared them with items of the same subscale obtained during the pandemic (COVID-19 surveys). Time in bed (TIB), measured in hours, was calculated from the reported wake up time and bedtime. Sleep onset latency was represented by the item 'I need __ minutes to fall asleep'. The number of awakenings was represented by 'After falling asleep, I wake up __ times during the night'.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Linear mixed models (LMM, R package 'lme4') and generalized linear mixed models (GLMM with Poisson distribution for awakenings); participant ID and ABCD site as random terms
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: assessment date (7 levels), pre-pandemic age, school (no school/school), time spent on social media platforms, time spent on single player video games, sex, race, ethnicity, parental education
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: The main analyses focused on examining changes in sleep measures (time in bed, bed time, sleep onset latency in minutes) as well as the relationship between sleep and screen time before and during the pandemic, using linear mixed models (LMMs, R package 'lme4'). All the models included assessment date (factor with 7 levels), pre-pandemic age (in months, at Year 2 assessment), school (no school/school), time spent on social media platforms (in minutes), time spent on single player video games (in minutes), sex (male/female), adjusting for race, ethnicity, and highest level of parental education. Participant ID and ABCD collection site were included as random terms.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: chi-squared from LMM likelihood ratio test
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: The time spent on social media platforms (chi2(1) = 114.56, p < .01) were significantly associated with TIB. Social media and video game playing were associated with shorter TIB. The slope of the social media-TIB relationship was steeper for the pre-pandemic assessment (simple slope_Pre-pandemic = -0.24, p < .01)

There were main effects for both social media use (chi2(1) = 456.52, p < .01), and video game time (chi2(1) = 74.03, p < .01) on bedtime, with longer screen time being associated with later bedtime. Social media use at pre-pandemic: simple slope = 0.19, p < .01
time spent on social media platforms was associated with longer sleep onset latency (chi2_social media(1) = 33.74, p <.01)
Social media use (chi2_social media use(1) = 6.94, p=.008) was associated with higher frequency of awakenings

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper reports chi-squared values and simple slopes from LMMs but does not report CIs for the primary screen-time-sleep associations. Effect sizes are not explicitly discussed in terms of magnitude.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: There is need to implement public policy that supports positive health behaviors, such as sleep, particularly in this cohort of adolescents. It would be beneficial to increase awareness and education directed to adolescents, to promote balanced and informed use of social media platforms, video games, and other screen usage. Clinicians should assess for screen time use and sleep during the pandemic and help adolescents with individual treatment plans when necessary.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses on pandemic-related changes in both screen time and sleep, and the relationship between them. It describes increased screen time and altered sleep patterns across the pandemic period, highlighting sex and age differences. The paper frames findings in the context of pandemic-specific disruptions rather than generalizing broadly about screen time harms. It appropriately notes that screen time served adaptive functions during the pandemic (maintaining social connections). Recommendations focus on balanced use and awareness.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: The paper reports chi-squared values from LMM likelihood ratio tests and simple slopes rather than standardized effect sizes or CIs, making it difficult to extract precise effect size estimates for the screen-time-sleep associations. The simple slopes are reported but it is not clear what the units represent after standardization.
